prompts.baseline:
Gary Marcus posted on X:

"Anyone expecting the current paradigm to be close to AGI is delusional."

How likely is it that AGI will be reached primarily through scaling and refining large language model architectures (by any lab or company), rather than through paradigm shifts to fundamentally different approaches, before 2035? Weigh the relevant considerations and respond with a single best-estimate probability.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 30%.**

I’d interpret the question as: *Before 2035, what is the chance that broadly human-level general AI is achieved mainly by continuing the current LLM/foundation-model trajectory — larger/better multimodal transformer-like models, better post-training, synthetic data, tool use, memory, agent scaffolding, inference-time reasoning — rather than by a fundamentally different paradigm?* On that interpretation, my estimate is **about one in three**.

Why that high:

- **The empirical trend is real.** Scaling language/foundation models has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected: coding, math, multimodal reasoning, tool use, instruction following, and cross-domain transfer.
- **The current paradigm has overwhelming momentum.** The best-funded labs, compute infrastructure, talent pipelines, and deployment loops are all centered on LLM-like foundation models. If AGI arrives before 2035, it is more likely to come from the paradigm already closest to generality.
- **“LLM refinement” can include a lot.** Long-context memory, retrieval, agents, self-play/synthetic data, multimodality, reinforcement learning, external tools, verifiers, and inference-time search may fix many present weaknesses without looking like a clean paradigm break.

Why not higher:

- **Current models still lack robust agency and reliability.** They hallucinate, fail at long-horizon planning, lack stable world models, struggle with continual learning, and can be brittle under distribution shift.
- **Scaling may hit bottlenecks.** High-quality data, compute, energy, chip supply, economics, and diminishing returns could slow progress.
- **AGI may require something qualitatively different.** Strong causal reasoning, autonomous scientific discovery, embodied learning, explicit planning architectures, or neurosymbolic/world-model systems may end up being central rather than auxiliary.
- **“Before 2035” is soon.** Even if the LLM-family path eventually works, aligning all the pieces into dependable AGI within nine or ten years is not guaranteed.

My rough decomposition would be: perhaps **45%** chance of AGI by 2035 under any approach, and conditional on that happening, around **65%** chance the winning system is primarily LLM/foundation-model-derived. That gives roughly **0.45 × 0.65 ≈ 30%**.

So I would not call expectations of LLM-path AGI “delusional,” but I would call them highly uncertain. My single best estimate: **30%**.

So my single best-estimate probability: **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’m interpreting this as: *by before 2035, some lab reaches a broadly recognized AGI-level system, and the core route is still recognizably the LLM/foundation-model paradigm—scaled, multimodal, tool-using, agentic, post-trained, perhaps with retrieval/memory/search/RL refinements—rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.*

Why I put it as high as **35%**:

- Scaling laws and post-training improvements have been surprisingly robust.
- LLM-family systems already show broad transfer across language, code, reasoning, tool use, and multimodal tasks.
- Many apparent weaknesses—memory, reliability, planning, factuality, tool use, long-horizon execution—may be addressable with engineering layers, inference-time compute, synthetic data, RL, verification, and agent scaffolding rather than a clean paradigm break.
- If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to grow out of today’s dominant foundation-model ecosystem than from a wholly separate approach.

Why I don’t put it above even odds:

- Current LLMs still struggle with robust causal reasoning, grounded world models, autonomy, continual learning, and long-horizon reliability.
- Data, compute, energy, and economics could impose diminishing returns before human-level generality.
- “AGI” may require architectural ingredients that are not just bigger/better language models—e.g. richer world models, embodied learning, symbolic abstraction, active experimentation, or fundamentally different training regimes.
- Benchmark gains may overstate real-world competence.

So I would not call expectation of LLM-path AGI by 2035 “delusional,” but I’d call it far from settled. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I interpret the question as: *By the start of 2035, will some lab build something reasonably called AGI, and will the main route be scaled/refined LLM-like foundation models rather than a fundamentally different paradigm?*

Why this is a serious possibility:

- LLMs have already shown unusually broad transfer across language, code, math, tool use, multimodal understanding, and interactive tasks.
- Scaling laws, better data curation, synthetic data, reinforcement learning, inference-time search, tool use, memory, and multimodality could plausibly close many gaps without abandoning the paradigm.
- The largest labs are investing overwhelmingly in this direction, so if AGI happens soon, path dependence favors the current paradigm.

Why I would not put it above even odds:

- Current systems still have major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounded understanding, and autonomous self-correction.
- “More scale” may face diminishing returns from data quality, compute cost, energy, chip supply, and evaluation bottlenecks.
- A system that is robustly human-level across domains may require deeper changes: explicit world models, different learning regimes, embodied interaction, neurosymbolic methods, or other architectures not well described as refined LLMs.
- The deadline—before 2035—is close enough that even rapid progress may not suffice.

So my view is: **not delusional, but far from certain. Around a one-in-three chance: 35%.**

- Economic incentives and global competition make intense scaling/refinement likely to continue.

So I would not call expectation of LLM-path AGI “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default-certainty outcome. My single best estimate is **40%**.

**40%**

I would count as “LLM-paradigm” not just bigger next-token predictors, but large multimodal transformer-like models refined with RL, synthetic data, retrieval, memory, tool use, agent scaffolds, verifiers, inference-time search, and code execution. Why not higher:

- Current systems are still unreliable in ways that matter for AGI: hallucination, brittle reasoning, poor long-horizon autonomy, weak causal grounding, and compounding error rates.
- Human-level generality may require active learning, persistent world models, embodiment, causal reasoning, or cognitive architectures that are not merely “scaled LLMs.”
- Data and compute may hit diminishing returns, especially if high-quality human data is exhausted and synthetic data does not fully substitute.
- Benchmark progress can overstate real-world robustness.

So I think Marcus’s “delusional” is too strong: an LLM-centric route to AGI by 2035 is a live possibility. But I would not make it the majority bet, because either AGI may arrive later than 2035 or require a deeper architectural shift.

**Best-estimate probability: 40%.**

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’m interpreting this as: before 2035, some lab achieves broadly human-level general cognitive capability—across reasoning, coding, research, planning, tool use, and learning—with the core route being scaled/refined LLM-like foundation models rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.

- Scaling plus post-training, synthetic data, inference-time search, memory, agents, multimodality, and tool use may be enough to remove many current weaknesses without a clean paradigm break.
- The dominant talent, compute, funding, and product feedback loops are overwhelmingly concentrated on LLM/foundation-model descendants.
- If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to come from the already-leading paradigm than from an unproven alternative that still needs to be invented, scaled, and industrialized.

Why the probability is not higher:

- Current systems remain brittle: hallucinations, weak long-horizon autonomy, poor causal grounding, limited continual learning, and unreliable planning are not solved merely by making models larger.
- Scaling may face diminishing returns from data limits, cost, energy, latency, and evaluation saturation.
- “AGI” may require architectural changes involving persistent world models, active learning, embodiment, symbolic/compositional reasoning, or memory systems that would count as more than just LLM refinement.
- Even if LLMs remain central, the decisive ingredient might be a new training or cognitive architecture such that calling it “mostly scaling LLMs” becomes misleading.

So I would not call belief in the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not make it the majority forecast. My single best estimate is **35%**.

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

My rough decomposition is:

- **Chance of something reasonably describable as AGI before 2035:** ~45–55%, depending heavily on definition.
- **Conditional chance that, if it happens by then, it is mainly via scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models rather than a clean paradigm break:** ~60–75%.

Why not higher?

- Current LLM-family systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon agency, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounding, and autonomous scientific/engineering work.
- Scaling may face bottlenecks: high-quality data, compute cost, energy, inference economics, and diminishing returns.
- “AGI” may require additions that are more than mere scaling: stronger memory, planning, tool use, reinforcement learning, world models, self-improvement loops, or embodied/interactive learning.

Why not lower?

- The empirical progress from scaling, post-training, multimodality, tool use, retrieval, agents, and test-time reasoning has been very strong.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is more likely to grow out of today’s foundation-model stack than from an entirely unrelated approach, simply because that stack has the talent, capital, infrastructure, and demonstrated capability gains.
- “Paradigm shift” is a high bar: many improvements that look novel may still be fairly described as refinements of the LLM/foundation-model paradigm.

So my single-number answer is: **about 35% likely**.

rows.answer:
Under a practical definition of AGI—systems that can autonomously perform most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly human level across domains—I would put the probability at:

**35%**

That assumes “LLM paradigm” includes transformer-like multimodal models with scaling, better post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, inference-time reasoning, and RL-style refinement. If “just bigger chatbots” is meant, the odds are much lower; if “LLM-centered systems with many engineering additions” is meant, the odds are meaningfully higher.

Why it is plausibly this high: current models keep gaining broad capabilities from scale and refinement; coding, math, multimodal understanding, tool use, and agentic workflows are improving quickly; and enormous compute, capital, and talent are being directed at exactly this path. If AGI arrives before 2035, it is quite plausible that it comes from descendants of today’s LLM systems.

Why it is not higher: current systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounding, and autonomous execution. It is not clear that more scale plus incremental architectural improvements will solve these rather than merely smooth over them. A genuinely new paradigm—e.g. stronger world-model-based RL, neuro-symbolic systems, embodied learning, or some currently unknown architecture—may be needed.

So my single best estimate: **35%**.

rows.answer:
My best estimate: **40%**.

I’m interpreting “primarily through scaling and refining LLM architectures” broadly: transformer-like/multimodal foundation models plus better post-training, RL, tool use, memory, agents, synthetic data, test-time search/reasoning, and architectural efficiency improvements—not merely “make a bigger chatbot.”

Why that high: current LLM-family systems have repeatedly absorbed capabilities once thought to require separate paradigms; scaling plus algorithmic refinement still appears productive; huge investment and talent are concentrated on this path; and if AGI arrives before 2035, the incumbent foundation-model paradigm is the likeliest launchpad.

Why not higher: current models remain unreliable on long-horizon autonomy, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounding, and self-correction; data/compute/economic constraints may bite; benchmark gains may overstate real generality; and “AGI” may require deeper architectural changes—e.g., more explicit world models, planning, embodied learning, or neurosymbolic mechanisms—than can fairly be called LLM refinement.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default-certainty route to AGI. **Single best-estimate probability: 40%.**

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 40%.**

I interpret “LLM architectures” broadly: transformer-like foundation models scaled and refined with multimodality, tool use, retrieval/memory, RL/post-training, inference-time search, synthetic data, agent scaffolding, and better training recipes—not merely “make a bigger next-token predictor.”

Why that probability is not tiny: the last decade’s evidence strongly favors the “bitter lesson”: broad capabilities have improved dramatically with scale, data, compute, and relatively incremental architectural/training refinements. LLM-centered systems have already absorbed coding, math, multimodal perception, tool use, and increasingly agentic workflows. There is no clear empirical wall yet, and investment plus AI-assisted R&D could keep pushing the paradigm faster than skeptics expect.

Why it is not higher: current systems still struggle with robust long-horizon planning, reliability, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounded understanding, and autonomous self-correction. Benchmarks may overstate generality, data/compute/power bottlenecks may bite, and “AGI” may require architectural ideas that are not just scaled LLMs with scaffolding. The closer the definition gets to fully autonomous, broadly reliable human-level agency, the more likely some deeper paradigm change becomes.

So I would not call LLM-scaling-to-AGI-before-2035 “delusional”; it is a serious live possibility. But I would also not make it the majority bet.

**Single best estimate: 40%.**

- Scaling has produced surprisingly general capabilities, and there is no clear evidence yet that the paradigm has hit a hard wall.

- Massive investment and talent concentration make it more likely that if AGI arrives soon, it comes from the dominant foundation-model track.

**35%.**

I’d interpret the question as: *By before 2035, will some lab reach broadly human-level general cognitive capability mainly by extending today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm—scaling, multimodality, RL, tool use, memory, agents, synthetic data, test-time compute—rather than by a clearly new paradigm?* Under that interpretation, my estimate is **about one in three**.

A rough decomposition:

- **P(AGI before 2035 by any route): ~45–55%**
- **P(LLM-descendant paradigm is the main route, conditional on AGI happening that soon): ~65–75%**
- Product: **~30–40%**, so I’d pick **35%**.

Why not lower: current LLMs already show unusually broad competence; scaling laws have been more reliable than many critics expected; tool use, code execution, multimodality, long-context memory, verifiers, RL, and agent scaffolding all look like natural extensions rather than paradigm breaks. Also, if AGI arrives within a decade, it is more likely to come from the heavily funded, rapidly iterated paradigm already in motion than from a wholly new one.

Why not higher: current systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal understanding, continual learning, grounded world models, and autonomous execution. Data, compute, energy, regulation, and diminishing returns could bite. And it remains plausible that robust AGI needs architectural ingredients not well captured by “scaled LLMs,” such as stronger world-modeling, explicit reasoning/search, neurosymbolic structure, or new forms of online learning.

So I would not call belief in the LLM-scaling path “delusional.” But I also would not treat it as the default near-certainty. My single best estimate: **35%**.

I would interpret the question as: *by 2035, will something plausibly called AGI be achieved, with the central technical route still being scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models—multimodal, tool-using, post-trained, agentic, with memory/retrieval/search—rather than a fundamentally different paradigm?* Under that interpretation, my estimate is **about 35%**.

- Current LLMs still show major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, autonomy, and robustness outside the training distribution.
- Scaling may face bottlenecks: data quality, compute cost, energy, inference expense, diminishing returns, and regulatory/safety slowdowns.
- “AGI” may require capabilities that are awkward to get from next-token-prediction-derived systems alone, especially if one demands consistently competent open-ended agency rather than impressive task performance.
- A future system might still use neural nets and language models but depend on enough new machinery—model-based RL, symbolic reasoning, persistent self-improving agents, embodied world models—that calling it “primarily LLM scaling” would be misleading.

- The current paradigm has repeatedly outperformed skeptical expectations; scaling plus post-training has produced broad, general capabilities, not just narrow pattern matching.
- Many apparent limitations can be partially addressed within the LLM/foundation-model paradigm: tool use, code execution, retrieval, long context, synthetic data, verifiers, self-play, multimodal training, and agent scaffolding.

- “Paradigm shift” is a high bar; many future changes may look like refinements rather than a clean break.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path to AGI by 2035 “delusional.” I’d call it **plausible but far from assured**: roughly **35%**.

rows.answer:
Assuming “AGI” means a system that can robustly perform or learn most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human-or-better level, not necessarily embodied or conscious, my best estimate is:

**35%**

My rough decomposition:

- **~50%** chance that something reasonably called AGI is achieved before 2035.
- Conditional on that happening, **~70%** chance it comes mainly from the current foundation-model/LLM trajectory: larger models, better data, multimodality, tool use, memory, agent scaffolding, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and inference-time search—rather than a wholly different paradigm.
- Multiplying those gives **~35%**.

Why not higher: current LLMs still have serious issues with reliability, causal/world modeling, long-horizon planning, continual learning, grounding, and autonomous agency; data/compute/economics/regulation could also bite. Some of these may require deeper architectural changes.

Why not lower: scaling has repeatedly produced broad, unexpected capability gains; LLM-centered systems are already the dominant research and commercialization path; many apparent weaknesses can plausibly be attacked with refinements rather than a clean paradigm break; and by 2035 there is time for several more generations of algorithmic and compute scaling.

So I would say Marcus-style confidence that the LLM paradigm is nowhere near sufficient is too strong—but confidence that it will deliver AGI soon is also too strong. My single-point estimate: **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’m interpreting this as: by Jan. 1, 2035, some lab develops a system that is broadly human-level or better across most cognitive tasks, and the main technical path is recognizably descended from today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm—larger/better multimodal models, post-training, RL, tool use, memory, agent scaffolding, inference-time compute, synthetic data, etc.—rather than a clearly new paradigm such as brain emulation, a symbolic cognitive architecture, or something fundamentally unlike LLMs.

Why the probability is substantial:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- LLMs already function as general-purpose interfaces to tools, code, search, reasoning traces, and multimodal inputs.
- Inference-time compute, self-play/RL, synthetic data, long-context memory, and agentic scaffolding could plausibly address many current weaknesses without a clean paradigm break.
- Enormous capital and talent are being directed at exactly this path.

Why I don’t put it above 50%:

- Current systems still show fragility in reasoning, planning, causal understanding, long-horizon autonomy, and reliability.
- Data/compute/economic bottlenecks may make “just scale/refine” deliver diminishing returns.
- Genuine AGI may require better continual learning, world modeling, agency, or grounding than transformer-style LLMs naturally provide.
- “AGI” is a moving and ambiguous target; impressive automation before 2035 may still fall short of robust general intelligence.

So I’d say Gary Marcus’s confidence that the current paradigm is nowhere near enough is too strong, but the opposite view—that LLM scaling/refinement will probably get us to AGI before 2035—is also too strong. My single best estimate is **35%**.

By that I mean: roughly a **one-in-three chance** that before 2035 we get something reasonably called AGI whose core route was **scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models**—including multimodality, tool use, agent scaffolding, memory, retrieval, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and test-time search—rather than a clean break to a fundamentally different paradigm.

My rough decomposition would be: **~50–55% chance of AGI before 2035 under a moderately demanding definition**, and conditional on that, **~60–70% chance it is primarily LLM/foundation-model-derived**. Multiplying gives a best guess around **35%**. So I would not call belief in the LLM route “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default with overwhelming confidence.

So my single best estimate is **35%**: roughly “one in three.” Marcus’s “delusional” framing seems too dismissive, but treating LLM scaling/refinement as the default guaranteed path to AGI by 2035 also seems too confident.

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I would put the chance that **AGI is reached before 2035 primarily via scaling/refining LLM-style architectures**—including multimodal transformers, better post-training/RL, tool use, memory, agents, synthetic data, and test-time reasoning—at roughly **one in three**.

- Current LLMs still show serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world-modeling, continual learning, causal reasoning, and autonomous goal pursuit.
- Data, compute, and energy constraints may slow straightforward scaling.
- “AGI” may require architectural ingredients that are not just scaled language modeling: persistent learning, richer world models, active exploration, robotics/embodiment, symbolic or model-based reasoning, or new training regimes.
- Benchmarks can be misleading; impressive local competence does not guarantee robust general agency.

Why not lower:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced capabilities that many skeptics expected would require qualitatively different systems.
- Transformer/LLM-like systems are unusually general: language, code, vision, audio, tool use, reasoning traces, and agentic scaffolding can all be absorbed into the same broad paradigm.
- The “LLM architecture” category is elastic: a future AGI may still be recognizably LLM-centered while adding memory, search, RL, self-play, verifiers, simulators, and multimodal grounding.
- If AGI happens before 2035, the default route is likely to build on the currently dominant, massively funded paradigm rather than come from an entirely separate approach.

So my estimate is:

> **~35% probability that AGI before 2035 comes primarily from scaled/refined LLM-style systems.**

A stricter definition of AGI would push that lower, perhaps toward **20–25%**; a looser “economically transformative AI assistant/agent” definition would push it higher, perhaps **45–55%**.

Under a broad reading—multimodal transformer-like foundation models, scaled further and refined with better post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, agent scaffolding, verification, and test-time reasoning—I’d put the probability at:

**35%**

Why not higher: current LLM-centered systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal understanding, continual learning, grounded world models, and autonomous execution. Scaling may keep helping, but it is not guaranteed to fix those gaps, and data/compute/evaluation bottlenecks could become binding.

Why not much lower: the last few years provide real evidence that general-purpose capability improves substantially with scale, post-training, tools, multimodality, and inference-time computation. Many things that looked like “missing ingredients” have turned out to be partially addressable within the foundation-model paradigm. If AGI arrives before 2035, it seems quite plausible that it will look like a heavily engineered descendant of today’s LLMs rather than a clean break.

So my single best estimate: **35% likely before 2035 via primarily scaled/refined LLM-style architectures.**

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 40%.**

I interpret this as: by 2035, some lab achieves broadly human-level or better general cognitive capability, and the core path is still recognizable as scaled/refined LLM/foundation-model architecture—e.g., multimodal transformers, synthetic data, RL/post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, agent scaffolding, test-time search—not a wholly different symbolic, neuromorphic, or non-LLM paradigm.

Why it could happen:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced broader-than-expected capabilities.
- LLM-like systems already show generality across language, code, reasoning, instruction following, and tool use.
- Many weaknesses may be reduced by refinements: better data, longer context, memory, planning loops, verifiers, multimodal grounding, autonomous tool use, and synthetic training environments.
- The investment and talent going into this paradigm are enormous.

Why I would not put it much higher:

- Current systems remain brittle: hallucination, weak long-horizon autonomy, poor causal robustness, limited continual learning, and unreliable reasoning.
- “AGI” may require forms of world modeling, active learning, embodiment, or planning that are not naturally obtained by scaling next-token prediction.
- Compute, data, energy, regulation, and economics may impose bottlenecks before robust AGI.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it may still rely on LLMs but require enough new machinery that calling it merely “scaling/refining LLMs” becomes misleading.

Roughly, I’d decompose this as: perhaps **55–60%** that AGI is reached by 2035 at all, and conditional on that, maybe **65–70%** that it is mostly through descendants of today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm. Multiplying gives a best estimate around **40%**.

So: **not “delusional,” but far from assured — 40%.**

- It is plausible that reliable AGI requires deeper advances in world modeling, continual learning, embodiment, symbolic reasoning, or agency architectures that are not just “better LLMs.”
- “AGI” itself may require a higher bar than economically useful chatbot/agent competence.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path to AGI before 2035 “delusional.” But I also would not treat it as the default-certainty outcome. My single best estimate is **about 40%**.
